meta.title: W140 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W140 engine — M104, M119, M120, OM603, OM606. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM603 "rod bender" risk explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes W140 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W140 engine typically costs £1,800–£12,000 supply only [MB‑QUOTE], depending on engine — the M104 straight‑six runs £1,800–£4,000, while the M120 V12 in the S 600 can cost £6,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £780–£1,430. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W140 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M104 3.2‑litre straight‑six (S 320) is the most dependable choice — it's a simple, robust engine with no major systemic weaknesses beyond normal head gasket wear. The M119 V8 is also reliable but watch the timing‑chain guides. The OM606 diesel (S 300 Turbodiesel, 1996–1998) is a significant improvement over the earlier OM603.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes W140?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM603 3.5‑litre diesels (1991–1993) suffer from connecting‑rod failure — known as the "rod bender." M119 V8s have timing‑chain guide wear; M120 V12s suffer from expensive coil pack and engine mount failures; M104 straight‑sixes can develop head gasket leaks. Regular servicing is critical across all engines.

It depends heavily on the engine. The M104 straight‑six and M119 V8 are generally robust if well‑maintained. The M120 V12 is an engineering masterpiece but demands expensive upkeep. The OM603 3.5‑litre diesel is the one to avoid due to its catastrophic connecting‑rod failure risk — the later OM606 is much safer. Overall, a well‑maintained W140 can be exceptionally durable.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W140 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM603 3.5‑litre turbo‑diesel (300 SD / S 350 Turbodiesel, 1991–1993) is the one we consistently advise against — its connecting‑rod failure reputation is well‑documented and can destroy the engine without warning. The M120 V12 isn't an "avoid" but requires deep pockets for maintenance.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W140 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs include: a knocking or tapping from the bottom end (OM603 rod failure), rattling on start‑up (M119 chain guides), misfires or rough running (M120 coil packs), or coolant loss with oil contamination (M104 head gasket). If you experience any of these, get a diagnosis immediately — early intervention saves thousands.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes W140 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and condition. Against a well‑kept £12,000 S 500, a £6,500 M119 recon is 54% of the car's value — safe if the car is otherwise exceptional. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon is 130% — walk away. Use our 70% rule as your guide. For the M120 V12, the calculus is different — many enthusiasts will pay for the rebuild due to the engine's rarity and desirability.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W140 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, the M104 straight‑six can exceed 250,000 miles, and the M119 V8 is similarly durable. The M120 V12 is robust but expensive to maintain; coil pack replacement is a recurring cost. The OM603 has a limited life due to its rod‑bender risk; the OM606 is capable of 200,000+ miles with regular servicing.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W140 and C140?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the same platform and many engines. The W140 is the 4‑door saloon (1991–1998, this page), while the C140 is the 2‑door coupé (1992–1998, later rebadged as the CL‑Class in 1996). Costs vary slightly by body style — see the C140's own Generation Page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W140 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and in the vehicle documentation. The code is stamped on the engine block — on M104/OM603/OM606 it's on the left side, on M119/M120 it's on the right side. Tell us your variant and registration year, and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: Mercedes S‑Class W140 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes S‑Class W140 Engine Codes — The Complete Database

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: All ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier, derived from cross‑referencing specialist and owner sources — confirm against the Model Page before publishing. The OM603 receives a lower rating due to documented connecting‑rod failure risks in early W140 applications.

overview.intro: The W140 is the third generation of the Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class, a flagship full‑size luxury saloon produced from April 1991 to September 1998. Unveiled at the Geneva International Motor Show in March 1991, it succeeded the W126 and introduced a V12 engine for the first time in a post‑war Mercedes‑Benz passenger car. The W140 is renowned for its over‑engineered build quality, advanced technology, and the legendary M120 V12 — developed after BMW's surprise V12 announcement forced Mercedes to delay the W140's launch by 18 months. The range included short‑ and long‑wheelbase saloons, with the coupé (C140) later spun off as the CL‑Class in 1996.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W140 • Predecessor: W126 • Successor: W220 • Sibling body styles in this generation: C140 Coupé (1992–1998, later CL‑Class).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No W140‑specific enquiry figure is available; the W140 remains a sought‑after classic, with the M120 V12 and M119 V8 variants generating the most enthusiast interest.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Six — more power, same reliability
V8 Refinement — smooth, watch the chain
V12 Legend — engineering icon, high running costs
High Risk — rod‑bender reputation, avoid unless proven
Best Diesel — robust four‑valve, much improved

economicsRule.title: The W140 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£6,500 M119 recon against a £5,000–£15,000 W140 sits at 23–130% of the car's value. On a sub‑£6,000 example, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £12,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 29–54% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For the OM603 diesel, the risk is not just cost — it's catastrophic failure.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W140 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M104 3.2 straight‑six — simple, robust, cheapest to maintain.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W140 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M120 V12 — coil packs, mounts, and specialist labour add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W140 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 500 (M119 5.0 V8) — V8 prestige without V12 running costs.

problems.title: OM603 Connecting‑Rod Failure (The "Rod Bender")
problems.rootCause: The OM603 3.5‑litre turbo‑diesel has a documented weakness in its connecting rods — colloquially known as the "rod bender." Catastrophic failure can occur without warning, often destroying the engine completely.
problems.recommendation: If you're considering a W140 with the OM603 3.5‑litre diesel, proceed with extreme caution — many specialists advise avoiding these cars entirely unless the engine has been fully rebuilt or replaced. The S 300 Turbodiesel (OM606, 1996–1998) is a much safer diesel alternative.

cta.label: Compare OM603 engine prices for your W140 →

problems.title: M119 Timing‑Chain Guide Wear
problems.rootCause: The timing‑chain guides on the M119 V8 can become brittle with age and heat cycling. If they fail, the chain can slip, leading to catastrophic valve‑to‑piston contact.
problems.recommendation: If your W140 V8 has over 100,000 miles and no documented timing‑chain guide replacement, budget for the work proactively — an £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £3,000+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare M119 engine prices for your W140 →

problems.title: M120 V12 Coil Pack & Mount Failure
problems.rootCause: The M120 V12 has 12 individual coil packs that can fail with age. Engine mounts also wear more quickly due to the engine's weight and vibration. Parts are expensive and often specialist‑only.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a W140 V12, budget for a full ignition system inspection — coil pack replacement is inevitable on higher‑mileage examples. This is the price of V12 ownership.

cta.label: Compare M120 engine prices for your W140 →

problems.title: M104 Head Gasket Failure
problems.affectedModels: W140 300 SE, S 280, S 320 (M104 straight‑six petrol).
problems.rootCause: The M104 straight‑six can develop head gasket leaks, particularly if the cooling system has been neglected. Early signs include coolant loss and oil contamination.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels closely and address any leaks immediately — a £600–£1,200 head gasket repair is far cheaper than the £1,800+ replacement that follows an overheat.

cta.label: Compare M104 engine prices for your W140 →
